Tier is appropriate: 'Probable' is correctly declared with candid acknowledgment of why 'Proved' is unattainable (no primary-informant sources, Ancestry records inaccessible). entry.rationale: This is the primary genealogical unknown in the objective. Mary's parents are not recorded in the tree. Baltimore vital records, the 1860 and 1870 census (Mary would be ~6 and ~16, likely still in the parental household), and Maryland church/civil records can plausibly name her parents. Answering this question first establishes the family context that may also clarify her marriage.

entry.rationale: Mary was born 10 July 1854; at age 5–6 she almost certainly lived in her parents' household. The tree already records a 1860 residence in the 8th District of Baltimore. The 1860 federal census household will name both parents (father and mother) and is the single highest-probability record for identifying them.

entry.rationale: Mary Crowther Prizer's 1920 North Carolina death certificate is already recorded in the tree sources (M3MH-D5J). Death certificates list the deceased's parents; even as secondary information this is direct evidence for the parentage question. This source needs formal extraction into assertions.

Mary would be age 15–16 in 1870 and likely still in her parents' household. The tree sources include a 1870 census reference (M91B-YFN, 'Manie Crowther'). This record needs formal extraction; if it records the full household it will corroborate or name the parents identified in the 1860 census.

The Maryland Births and Christenings Index, 1662–1911 (Ancestry collection 2565) covers Baltimore church baptisms. A baptism entry for Mary Crowther born 10 July 1854 would name her father and possibly her mother, constituting primary information for parentage. This is the closest equivalent to a birth certificate for this period.

Per BCG parentage planning standards, a dedicated search for the parents' marriage to each other is required. Maryland Compiled Marriages 1667–1899 (Ancestry collection 4729) and the Index to Marriages in the Baltimore Sun 1851–1860 (collection 48588) cover this period. The parents' marriage record provides the mother's maiden name (omitted from census and death records) and, by its date relative to Mary's 1854 birth, constitutes direct evidence of paternity.

Mary's own marriage record will name her father (and often her mother) and confirm her surname at marriage. This also directly advances q_002 (whom she married). FamilySearch holds Maryland and New York marriage records; the children's surnames (Prizer) suggest she married a Prizer, possibly in Baltimore or Rochester, NY before 1886 (eldest child born Nov 1886).

Mary would be age 25–26 in 1880. If she had not yet married by that date, she may still appear in her parents' household, allowing corroboration of parent names. Even if she had left, her parents may appear as a couple — confirming the family unit. Fallback for the 1860 census if the 1860 household is not found.

The Index to Marriages and Deaths in the Baltimore Sun 1837–1850 and 1851–1860 (Ancestry collections 48587, 48588) may record the parents' marriage announcement, naming both parties. Chronicling America also holds Baltimore papers. A newspaper marriage notice can corroborate and sometimes supply more detail than the marriage register. FAN item: searches associates of the Crowther family in Baltimore.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Parents of Mary Crowther (218N-WSD)

**Tier: Probable**

### Conclusion

Mary Crowther (born 10 July 1854/55, Baltimore, Maryland; died 22 February 1920, Southern Pines, North Carolina) was the daughter of **John Crowther** (born ca. 1832, Maryland) and his first wife **Fanny E. (Bosley) Crowther** (born ca. 1834, Maryland). Three independent records corroborate the father; two independent records establish the mother’s maiden surname by correlation, with a third indirect corroboration.

### Evidence for the Father

The **1880 U.S. Federal Census** (derivative, FamilySearch, accessed 8 July 2026) enumerates Mary Crowther in John Crowther’s Baltimore City household with an explicit ParentChild relationship — direct evidence, as 1880 was the first U.S. census year to include a formal relationship column. John Crowther (head, born ca. 1832 Maryland, Farmer) and Mary Crowther (daughter, born ca. 1855 Maryland, Single, At Home) are linked by this structural relationship. Informant quality is indeterminate — the respondent is unknown — but the most plausible respondent, the household head John Crowther himself, would have first-hand knowledge of his own children. (Src_004; assertions a_038–a_042; person evidence pe_042–pe_047.)

Independently, the **North Carolina Death Certificate** for Mary Prizer (née Crowther), filed 22 February 1920 (derivative, FamilySearch), explicitly names “John Crowther Jr” as the decedent’s father, with birthplace “Baltimore Co., Md.” — direct evidence for paternity. The personal informant was almost certainly Edward Prizer (husband), whose proximity to Mary’s birth is secondary (he was not present). The “Jr” suffix suggests a naming convention distinguishing John from his own father or a son; it does not contradict the identification. (Src_002; assertion a_025; person evidence pe_027–pe_028.)

A third independent record, the **1860 U.S. Federal Census** (derivative, FamilySearch), places Mary Crowther (born ca. 1854, Maryland) in sequential enumeration after John Crowther (born ca. 1832, Maryland) and Fanny E. Crowther (born ca. 1834, Maryland) in Baltimore’s 8th District. The 1860 census lacks a relationship column, so this is indirect evidence — the household position is consistent with a parent-child relationship but not conclusive in isolation. (Src_001; assertion a_005; person evidence pe_005.)

### Evidence for the Mother

The mother’s identity emerges from the correlation of two independent records and a third indirect element:

First, the **NC death certificate** names the decedent’s mother’s maiden surname as “Bosley,” birthplace “Baltimore Co., Md.” — direct evidence for the maiden surname, from secondary informant Edward Prizer. (Src_002; assertion a_027; person evidence pe_030–pe_031.)

Second, the **1860 census** shows Fanny E. Crowther (John’s wife, born ca. 1834 Maryland) enumerated within a household headed by **William Bosley** (born ca. 1793, Maryland). William Bosley’s age and surname are consistent with his being Fanny’s father, placing her maiden name in the Bosley family — indirect evidence connecting the 1860 wife’s name to the surname stated on the 1920 death certificate. (Src_001; assertions a_010, a_012; person evidence pe_010–pe_012.)

Third, the **1880 census** records a son named **Frank Bosley Crowther** (born ca. 1874). The middle name “Bosley” follows the 19th-century custom of honoring the maternal grandmother’s family in a child’s given name — indirect corroboration, independent of the death certificate, that Bosley was the maternal family surname. (Src_004; assertion a_043; person evidence pe_048.)

Fanny E. Crowther’s given name and married surname come from the 1860 census; her maiden surname comes from the NC death certificate via correlation through the Bosley household. No single record names “Fanny Bosley” as marrying John Crowther — this is the principal limitation of the mother’s identification.

### Conflict Noted

Mary’s birth year is 1854 in the family tree but 1855 in both the NC death certificate and the 1880 census. A 1-year discrepancy is common in secondary sources reporting births that occurred decades earlier (the death cert informant reporting ca. 65 years after the event) and in census age recording. This conflict is immaterial to the parentage question.

### Limitations

Maryland county marriage records pre-1898 are not indexed on FamilySearch (confirmed by searches log_005 and log_006). The parents’ marriage record — which would directly name “Fanny Bosley” marrying John Crowther and provide the strongest corroboration for the mother’s identification — resides in Ancestry’s Maryland Compiled Marriages 1667–1899 (collection 4729), which was not searched due to the absence of an Ancestry subscription. Mary’s baptism record (Ancestry Maryland Births and Christenings, collection 2565) was also not searched. These records are the most direct path to advancing this conclusion from Probable to Proved.

### Tier Declaration

**Probable.** Three independent sources consistently identify John Crowther as Mary’s father; two records with direct evidence and a third with indirect evidence, with no contradicting evidence found. The mother’s maiden surname Bosley is established by direct statement (death cert) corroborated by 1860 household context and naming-convention evidence. The conclusion is limited from Proved by: (1) the absence of primary-information sources — all informant quality is secondary or indeterminate; (2) the unsearched parents’ marriage record on Ancestry, which is the named decisive record for a parentage conclusion.

The 1920 NC death certificate (extracted in pli_002 for q_001) names 'Edward Prizer' as Mary's husband, providing direct primary-information evidence of the marriage. A dedicated question is needed to formally research the marriage: confirm the identity of Edward Prizer, find the marriage record (date, place, witnesses), and write a GPS-compliant proof conclusion. The FamilySearch marriage search (pli_006, log_005) was already attempted with negative results — Maryland pre-1898 county marriage records are not indexed on FamilySearch. Ancestry's Maryland Compiled Marriages 1667-1899 (collection 4729) and New York marriage records (if the marriage occurred after the family relocated) are the primary remaining repositories. ## Who Did Mary Crowther Marry?

Mary Crowther (born 10 July 1854, Baltimore, Maryland; died 22 February 1920, Southern Pines, Moore County, North Carolina) married **Edward Prizer**, born March 1856 in Pennsylvania, approximately **1883**. Two independent sources from different record types and different informants confirm this conclusion.

### Source 1 — North Carolina Death Certificate, 1920

Mary's death certificate, filed in Moore County, North Carolina ("North Carolina, Death Certificates, 1909–1975," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:F34X-5XB, accessed 2026-07-08) lists the deceased as **Mary Prizer** and names **Edward Prizer** as her husband. The personal informant is identified as Edward Prizer — providing primary-quality information about the spousal relationship. The certificate's listing of her married surname as "Prizer" is consistent with all other evidence (a_019, a_029; person-evidence pe_021, pe_033).

### Source 2 — United States Federal Census, 1900

The 1900 United States Federal Census ("United States, Census, 1900," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:MSJW-8MY, accessed 2026-07-08) enumerates a household at Rochester Ward 12 ED 83, Monroe County, New York, headed by **Edward Prizer** (born March 1856, Pennsylvania, Married) with **Mary Prizer** (born July 1856, Maryland, Married) as wife. The record carries an explicit Couple relationship with a **Marriage fact dated 1883** — derived from the census "years married" column, which the couple most likely reported themselves. The five children enumerated in the household (Edward L., b. Oct 1885; John C., b. Nov 1886; Mary C., b. Jun 1888; Elizabeth, b. Oct 1894; William S., b. Feb 1895) are all born in New York, consistent with a marriage in 1883 followed by residence in Rochester, NY (a_052–a_070; person-evidence pe_057–pe_078).

### Cross-Person Consistency

Mary Prizer on the 1900 census is the research subject: her birth month (July) exactly matches Mary Crowther's documented birth of 10 July 1854 Baltimore; her birthplace (Maryland) is consistent with all prior sources; and Rochester, Monroe County, New York is her established 1900 residence. The 2-year birth-year discrepancy (1856 vs. 1854) is a common census self-report rounding error and does not impair identification. The five Prizer children are corroborated by tree persons LJRR-T9D, LJRR-TSY, LJRR-T7B, and LJRY-9PY (confirmed by exact birth-month matches) and newly identified I16 (Edward L., no prior tree record).

### Marriage Date and Place

The marriage occurred approximately 1883, per the 1900 census years-married column (17 years married as of 1900). The eldest child Edward L. Prizer (born October 1885) is consistent with this date. The marriage location is not established: Maryland marriages pre-1898 are not indexed on FamilySearch (nil at log_005, log_006), and the Ancestry Maryland Compiled Marriages database (collection 4729) is inaccessible due to subscription unavailability. Maryland or New York are both plausible jurisdictions.

### Tier Declaration

The conclusion is rated **Probable**. Two independent sources from different record types confirm Edward Prizer as Mary's husband (Proved-level evidence correlation), but the primary marriage record remains inaccessible, preventing full GPS closure. The NC death certificate's primary-quality informant (Edward Prizer himself) is the strongest single piece of evidence. No conflicting evidence was found across any source consulted.
